name: Liam Holt
title: Associate Professor
lab_synopsis: The Holt Lab studies how the physical state of the cell interior shapes information flow, with an emphasis on macromolecular crowding, intracellular pH, and biomolecular condensation. Using quantitative cell biology and synthetic biology, the group tests how phase separated condensates modulate kinase driven phosphoregulation, expand substrate specificity, and convert biophysical cues into chemical signals. In parallel, the lab investigates stress and nutrient signaling in yeast, including how mTORC1 tunes ribosome abundance to control cytoplasmic diffusion and phase separation, and how carbon starvation drives pH dependent transcriptional reprogramming through SWI SNF recruitment. Across these programs, the lab builds and applies genetically encoded multimeric nanoparticles for in cell microrheology and single particle tracking, combines these measurements with genome wide expression profiling and reconstituted biochemical assays, and integrates mechanistic modeling and simulations to link molecular crowding and condensation to gene regulation and signaling.
common_experiments_raw:
GEMs-Based Microrheology
* Genetically encoded nanoparticle tracking to measure intracellular viscoelasticity, crowding, and active matter across organelles
Mechanical Perturbation Systems
* Compression devices, tunable synthetic hydrogels, and osmotic manipulation to control cellular mechanical environment
Live-Cell Single-Particle Tracking
* High-throughput fluorescence microscopy with computational analysis of molecular diffusion and dynamics
Synthetic Biology & Protein Engineering
* Design of synthetic condensates (SynDrops), engineered transcription factors, and AI-designed proteins
Real-Time Metabolic Biosensors
* ATP and pH sensors for live monitoring of cellular energetics
research_keywords: Biomolecular condensates and phase separation as regulators of kinase signaling and phosphoregulation
Macromolecular crowding as a tunable cellular signal and determinant of reaction rates and diffusion
mTORC1 control of cytoplasmic biophysics through ribosome concentration and mesoscale crowding
Genetically encoded multimeric nanoparticles for high throughput microrheology and particle tracking in living cells
Carbon starvation and intracellular pH dynamics as signals driving transcriptional reprogramming in yeast
SWI SNF chromatin remodeling recruitment controlled by a pH sensitive low complexity domain in SNF5
Synthetic condensate design rules, scaffold flexibility, and client recruitment controlling signaling efficiency
Quantitative integration of imaging, biochemistry, and modeling to connect cell physics to gene regulation
experimental_capabilities: Live cell fluorescence microscopy, time lapse imaging, and quantitative image analysis
Single particle tracking, microrheology, and diffusion measurements using genetically encoded nanoparticles
Synthetic biology for engineered condensates, multivalent scaffolds, and phosphoregulation reporters
Yeast genetics and nutrient or stress perturbation assays
RNA seq and genome wide transcriptional profiling under starvation and stress conditions
In vitro chromatin remodeling and nucleosome remodeling assays with purified SWI SNF components and activators
In vitro kinase assays and phosphorylation readouts for condensate dependent signaling
Biophysical modeling and simulations of low complexity domains and crowding driven conformational changes
Microfabrication and microfluidics for controlled confinement and physical perturbations, including pressure measurements
model_systems: Saccharomyces cerevisiae for nutrient stress, pH sensing, chromatin remodeling, and cytoplasmic biophysics
Mammalian cultured cells for engineered condensates and kinase signaling assays
Purified biochemical systems for nucleosome remodeling and controlled kinase phosphorylation measurements
specialty_areas: Biophysics of signaling / biomolecular condensates / gene regulation under stress
keywords: Biomolecular condensates and phase separation as regulators of kinase signaling and phosphoregulation
Macromolecular crowding as a tunable cellular signal and determinant of reaction rates and diffusion
